editionStmt.edition: Digital Critical Edition of the Chapter Summaries (Capitula) to Exodus in the Tours Bibles

publicationStmt.p:
Welcome to the digital critical edition project of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles!
The editions are part of the PRIN 2022 PNRR project DOBiPS – Data Oriented Biblical Paratext Studies, awarded for the 2023–2025 period to the research units at the Universities of Udine (P.I. E. Colombi) and Cassino (P.I. R. Casavecchia), under the competitive PRIN PNRR call – National Recovery and Resilience Plan, Mission 4 "Education and Research", funded by the European Union’s NextGenerationEU programme (protocol no. P2022ZW4AW).
The Capitula prefixed to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles have been encoded in XML/TEI and published using the software EVT (Edition Visualization Technology).
The editions were prepared by members of the Udine research unit: Emanuela Colombi, Margherita Filippozzi, and Silverio Franzoni.
Details of the encoding choices and related methodological reflections have been presented in the working paper: E. Colombi, M. Filippozzi, S. Franzoni, The Digital Critical Edition of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles, DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.16761173.
Information on the individual editions is available by clicking the 'info' button at the top of the left-hand pane.

bibl.author: de Bruyne, Donatien
bibl.title: Sommaires, divisions et rubriques de la Bible latine

bibl.author: Bischoff, Bernhard
bibl.title: Katalog der festländischen Handschriften des neunten Jahrhunderts (mit Ausnahme der wisigotischen)

bibl.author: Quentin, Henri
bibl.title: Mémoire sur l'établissement du texte de la Vulgate. 1re partie: Octateuque

listWit.witness: text of the A series as printed by D. de Bruyne

tagsDecl.rendition: uncertainty whether in the scribe’s hand or added later

front.p:
This digital edition presents the reconstruction of the capitula of Series A (according to Donatien de Bruyne's classification ) that precede the Book of Exodus, in the textual form known from the Tours scriptorium.
To enhance the clarity of the critical apparatus, certain graphical and commentary devices have been adopted:
– In the reconstructed text, features characteristic of the Turonian textual form are highlighted in comparison with that of the Maurdramnus Bible (represented by manuscript Amiens, Bibliothèques d'Amiens Métropole, Ms. 6, Corbie, 771-783 = Am1) and the Tours Octateuch (Tours, Bibliothèque municipale, 10, Tours, 790–780 = Tu).
Bold text indicates adiaphoric readings, i.e. readings equivalent to those attested in Am1 and/or Tu; italicised terms signal readings that are clearly erroneous, either exclusive to the Tours Bibles or shared with Am1 or (more often) with Tu. These readings have been included in the main text, as the aim is to reconstruct the form of the capitula at the origin of the Tours Bibles.
– Additionally, in the left window displaying the reconstructed text, clickable red dots provide brief annotations indicating whether the reading is adiaphoric (adiaph.) or erroneous (error), and whether it is attested only in the Tours Bibles (Φ) or also in Am1 or Tu. The opposing witness is introduced with the abbreviation “vs”.
Innovations of Am1 or Tu that are not shared with the Tours Bibles are not specifically marked, but can be identified through the apparatus displayed in the right-hand window.
– Finally, the critical apparatus includes notes, accessible via the "Critical note" tab when present, which comment where necessary on textual choices and the possible genesis of certain variants, with particular attention to those related to the Old Latin text of Exodus, as attested in the Codex Lugdunensis (Lyon, Bibliothèque municipale, 403, 6th cent.) or, where this is lacunose, as conjectured on the basis of the LXX or of patristic quotations.
These features allow users to quickly grasp the most salient textual phenomena and their philological implications.
It is possible, first of all, to conjecture a common archetype prior to the Maurdramnus Bible, the Tours Octateuch, and all the Tours Bibles, responsible for the incongruous addition feram comestam (but commixtum in Tu) non manducand[]* in chapter 136, for which no plausible explanation appears in any biblical version.
Direct dependence of Φ on Am1 or Tu can be ruled out, since both of the latter exhibit irreversible corruptions absent from Φ.
No closer relationship seems to exist between the Maurdramnus Bible and the Tours Bibles: the occurrence of shared innovations against Tu – whether erroneous or adiaphoric – is very limited and insignificant (ch. 23, 32, 56, 58), that can be easily modified or emended by conjecture.
Innovations shared by Φ and Tu are likewise generally of limited significance, though somewhat more frequent (ch. 6, 9, 18, 45–46, 52, 122, 124: adiaphoric readings; iiii plaga, ch. 49, 74, 75: errors). Of these, only the erroneous abtaverit (rendered aptaverit in some Tours copies) for abortaverit in ch. 49 appears to carry some conjunctive weight. Also noteworthy – even though it likely reflects the original text — is the ordering of capitula 45 and 46, which follows the Old Latin sequence, while the Maurdramnus Bible (and de Bruyne’s edition) reflects the Vulgate order, which reverses them.
On the basis of the evidence provided by the capitula, it is difficult to take a firm position on the controversial question of the relationship between the Tours Octateuch and the Tours Bibles. The former, dated to 790–800, is considered by Henri Quentin (pp. 282–286) a preliminary stage with respect to the later Alcuinian Bibles; Bonifatius Fischer (pp. 125, 128, 256 et passim), however, stresses the need for a systematic comparison with the earlier Alcuinian Bibles, which might clarify — at least for the books of the Octateuch — whether a direct dependence of the Tours Bibles on Tu as a 'trial copy' of the Alcuinian revision is plausible.
In the chapter headings to the Book of Exodus, no unambiguous separative errors can be identified in Tu that would exclude such dependence. However, the frequency of minor innovations in Tu — often easily emendable by conjecture, though some appear to carry a certain weight (e.g. ch. 31, 96, 115, 134, 135) — rather suggests the existence of a common exemplar from which both Tu and a likely ancestor of the other Tours Bibles descend.
This ancestor would be responsible for a certain number of exclusive adiaphoric innovations (ch. 5, 11, ii. plaga, 10, 34, 59, 112, 126) and for some minor but evident errors (ch. 8, viii commandment, ch. 59, 96), none of which have been corrected in the surviving Tours copies.
In support of this derivation from a shared exemplar is also the absence of innovations that would define sub-groups within the Tours Bibles. The only notable exception is the oldest witness, St. Gallen, Stiftsbibliothek, 75 (Sg1), which stands out for numerous innovations not shared with the other Tours Bibles — some irreversible — such as normalisations to the Vulgate and omissions of the final words in many capitula. These did not give rise to a textual tradition in Tours but did so in St. Gallen, where the manuscript arrived shortly after its production. One of the hands that annotated the manuscript there radically altered the text of several chapters, possibly on the basis of collation with another, now lost, exemplar. The Grosse Hartmut Bibel (St. Gallen, Stiftsbibliothek, 77) likely descends from the Tours exemplar, incorporating both its original innovations and the corrections subsequently applied.

app.note: The reading in flumen proici, adopted in text by de Bruyne, reflects a normalisation to the Vulgate (Ex 1:22). The reading found in the consulted witnesses corresponds instead to the Vetus Latina, as attested by the text of the Codex Lugdunensis (Lyon, BM, 403 = VL 100).

app.lem: De puero quem inuenit filia pharaonis
Mortuo pharaone exaudiuit dominus filios israhel

app.note: The reading paruit, preserved in the Maurdramnus' Bible and in the Tours Octateuch ante correctionem, is probably the original one, as it matches the text of Ex 3:2 in the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100)

title.note: Φ praeter Sg1 + Tu (error) vs Am1

app.note: The reading socerum suum in the Maurdramnus Bible corresponds to Ex 4:18 in the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100). The Vulgate has cognatum suum

The reading uadam found in Sg1 and P1, accepted as attested by the oldest witness and correct, is nevertheless likely a normalization based on the Vulgate text (Ex 4:18: dixitque ei vadam et revertar ad fratres meos) of the erroneous uade attested in the other Turonian Bibles, probably influenced by the analogous verb in the following chapter. The reading uado found in the Maurdramnus’ Bible and in the Tours Octateuch may be either a variant or a simplification (possibly reflecting the original form) of the future ibo, which is the translation of the Greek πορεύσομαι in the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100).

title.note: Φ (error) praeter Sg1 P1 vs Am1 Tu

app.lem: Mortuo pharaone dixit dominus ad moysen: uade in
Moysi dicit dominus ut loquatur ad populum

app.note: The reading found in Z a.c., B2, and Bm is likely a normalization based on the Vulgate text (Ex 7:1: constitui te Deum pharaonis), as opposed to the Vetus Latina reading in VL 100, which follows the Septuagint: Dedi te in Deum pharaoni.
the anomalous form 'facta' instead of 'factus' is found also in Rufin., Orig. Hom. in Ex. 4:1
fecerunt et (Ex 7:11) in Codex Lugdunensis, fecerunt etiam in the Vulgate

The variants found in Sg1 appear to be normalizations based on the Vulgate text (Ex 7:11: uocauit autem pharao sapientes et maleficos et fecerunt etiam ipsi per incantationes aegyptias), and rule out the dependence of the other Turonian Bibles on this exemplar.

app.lem: Vbi aqua fluminis facta est sanguis

app.note: Similiter is attested both in the Vulgate and in the Septuagint (ὡσαύτως); the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) has a lacuna at this point.

app.lem: Percussit aaron in limo terrae et facti sunt

title.note: Φ (error) praeter Sg1 Z + Tu vs Am1

item.title: Mortem mittit dominus in omnia omni pecora aegyptiorum ȩgyptiorum

De fauilla quam sparsit moyses in caelum et facta sunt ulcera
uulnera The reading in Bm is a normalization based on the Vulgate text (Ex 9:10: sparsit illud Moses in caelum factaque sunt vulnera uesicarum turgentium); ulcera is likely the Vetus Latina translation of the Greek φλυκτίδες; cf. e.g. Ambr., off. 3.15.92: Iactauerunt fauillam in caelum et facta sunt ulcera et uesicae candentes.
in homines
Extendit Moyses manum in caelum cȩlum et pluit dominus ignem et grandinem
Venit locusta lucusta locusta lucusta super omnes fines aegypti ȩgypti

Factae facte factȩ sunt tenebrae tenebrȩ in tota terra totam terram
Φ Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1
aegypti
triduo

Dicit dominus moysi moysen ad moysen
adhuc unam plagam ego induco induo induco
in
pharaonem pharaone

Loquitur dominus moysi ad moysi ad moysen
Φ praeter Sg1 + Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 et aaron de sacramento paschae phaschae paschȩ

app.lem: plaga in qua percussit dominus omne

app.note: It cannot be ruled out that the reading primitiuum found in the Tours Octateuch may derive from a Vetus Latina version, since the same term used in the Septuagint, πρωτότοκον, is translated as primitiu* in several places in the Psalms (77:51, 134:8; 135:10) and below in ch. XXI for Ex 13:1.

item.title:
Habitauerunt filii israhel in aegypto annis annos
quadringentis triginta CCCCXXX quadringentos triginta
annis
Legem paschae phaschae phascae dat dominus
Dicit dominus ut sanctificent sanctificarent omne primitiuum

app.lem: Quinta progenie progeniae progeniȩ ascenderunt filii israhel de aegypto egypto terra aegypti also in the Septuagint we read ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου (Ex 13:18)
app.rdg: Quinta progenie ascenderunt filii israhel de aegypto

De egressione filiorum israhel ex egypto et translatione ossium ioseph At the current state of research, it appears impossible to determine the origin and any possible source of the substitute textual form found in Sg1, which is attributable to a ninth-century hand, probably from St. Gall. It cannot be ruled out that there was a perceived need to replace the expression quinta progenie, which corresponds to the text of the Septuagint but is absent in the Vulgate. The mention of the translation of Joseph’s bones, carried by Moses at the time of the exodus from Egypt (Ex 13:19), is found in other capitula series, and also in an alternative form of series A (as attested by the manuscript Bruxelles, KBR, 2, 12th c.), though expressed differently as an independent title: afferuntur ossa Ioseph de Aegypto.The alternative capitulum in the form attested in Sg1 is also found in the manuscript Sankt Gallen, SB, 77 (850–833, Grosse Hartmut Bibel), which proves that the replacement in Sg1 must predate that date.

app.witDetail: The corrections of numbering in Sg1 cease

item.title:
Consecutus est pharao filios filio filios israhel et cooperuit operuit cooperit
Φ Am1 (adiaph.) vs Tu
aegyptios ȩgyptios mare canticum

list.item: XXV XVIIII XXIIII Cantauit et maria cum mulieribus in tympanis tymphanis et choris

XXVI XX XXV
Murmurauit Murmorauit populus in deserto pro aqua

item.title: Murmurauerunt Murmurauerunt Murmorauerunt Murmurauerunt murmurauit et pro carnibus
Dedit dominus populo coturnices cotornices coturnices cotornices et mannam manna
Manducauerunt mannam manna annis quadraginta XL
Murmurauerunt pro aqua et eduxit de petra aquam aquam de petra

Venit amalech ad pugnam et percussit eum iosue
iosiae chis

Venit iethro ietro iotar The reading of Tu probably reflects the Old Latin translation of the Greek Ιοθορ; see also Aug. Loc. in Hept. 2, 92.
Φ Am1 (adiaph.?) vs Tu ad moysen in desertum

Consilium dedit iethro ietro iotar
Φ Am1 (adiaph.?) vs Tu
moysi moysy ut praeponeret praeceperat
tribunos tribunus
et centuriones

Testatur dominus per moysen populo ut audiant audiat audiunt
Φ (adiaph.) vs Am1 vs Tu
eum eam

Descendit dominus in montem monte
sina syna sinai in igne igni ignem igne ignem

Praecepit dominus moysi moysy ut ascendat in montem solus solus et aaron The replacement variant in Sg1, introduced by the near-contemporary hand previously discussed, appears to refer to a different moment: whereas in Ex 19:20 Moses ascends alone to the top of Mount Sinai (the adjective solus is not explicit in the biblical text, but is evident from the narrative), in Ex 19:24 the Lord commands: ascendesque tu et Aaron tecum. solis

Decem Decim Decem Decim uerba legis quae quam quem
praecepit praecipit dominus custodiri

app.note: written in vertical (one letter per line) between marginal numbers and text

item.title: Non erunt tibi dii alii alii dii dii alii absque me
Non facias facies tibi idolum iodolum neque ullam aliam similitudinem
Non sumes nomen dei tui in uanum uanunum
In mente habe diem sabbatorum
Honora patrem tuum et matrem
Non occides
Non moechaberis mȩchaberis moechabiris
Non furtum facies
Non dicis dices dicis dices Φ (error) vs Am1 Tu falsum testimonium

Non concupisces uxorem rem uxorem proximi tui neque aliquid aliquit
eius ei

Timuit populus
populus et dixerunt ad moysen: Tu loquere ad nos
Deos argenteos uel aureos non faciendos

De puero puer
hebraeo hebreo ebraeo hebrȩo empto
De filia distracta
De percusso et mortuo
Si quis occiderit aliquem dolo
Qui percusserit patrem aut matrem

item.note: Φ Tu vs Am1 (order of chapters according to VL)

lem.title: Qui maledixerit patri aut uel matri

app.note: The inversion of chapters 45 and 46 in the Maurdramnus’ Bible corresponds to the order of the prescriptions as found in the Vulgate (Ex 21:15-17). The Octateuch of Tours and the Turonian Bibles follow the order of the Vetus Latina as attested by the Codex Lugdunensis, which corresponds to the Septuagint.

item.note: Φ Tu vs Am1 (order of chapters according to VL)

lem.title:
Qui furto furtum
ereptum erepto
uendiderit uindiderit

item.title:
Si in
rixa percusserit aliquis aliquid lapide aut pugno

app.note: number LIII in Sg1 is aligned with 'et mortuus' written in the second line and consequently the chapter has two numbers.

item.title:
Si seruum seruo seruum
aut et an ancillam percusserit aliquis et mortuus fuerit

Si mulierem percusserit et abtauerit aptauerit aptauerit abortiuum fecerit abortauerit
Φ (error) Tu vs Am1
Oculum Oculus pro oculo

Oculum serui aut ancillae ancille ancillȩ ancillam
qui eiecerit quaeieserit eiecerit
Cornu Si cornua Si cornu Φ Tu (error) vs Am1 (error) aliquem percusserit taurus
Si foueam fodeam cisternam The Codex Lugdunensis presents the term lacum as translation of the Greek λάκκον (Ex 21:33). quis foderit

Si taurus taurus bos
aliud aliquid alium
Φ (error) praeter Sg1 (error) vs Am1 Tu
cornu percusserit percusserit tauro

rdg.title: quod sciuit dominus bouem corniperam
rdg.note: added by a nearly contemporary hand in the upper margin (Ex 21:36: cornipeta).

item.title: Si quis uitulum uitulum bouem aut ouem inuolauerit
De furo furo fure furto furo fure Φ Am1 (error) vs Tu percusso
De agro alieno aliendo depasto
De area incensa uel messis messis messe messe messe Φ Am1 (error) vs Tu
De commendato et furtum furtim Φ (error) vs Am1 Tu ab aliquo erepto ereptum reperto
De iumento uel aliquo pecude commendato et mortuo
Si petierit percusserit αἰτήσῃ in the Septuagint (Ex 22:13) quis animal et mortuum fuerit
De uirgine nondum sponsata
Maleficum occidendum
Qui dormierit cum pecude occidendum
Qui immolauerit diis eradicandum
Aduenam non nocendum
Viduam et orfanum orfanos orfanos orfanum orphanum non nocendos

De fenore foenore
et
Φ (adiaph.) vs Am1 Tu de pignore
Non blasphemandum blaphemandum blasphemandum
Primitias offerendas
Omne primitiuum primitium domino offerendum

Carnem a bestia
laceratam add. in marg. P8 lacerata
non edendam edendum edendum edendam
With some hesitation, the addition 'lacerata', found in several Turonian Bibles, was not adopted due to its non-unanimous attestation and its absence from both the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Tours Octateuch. However, it is worth noting that the term is closer to the Septuagint’s θηριάλωτον than to the Vulgate’s corresponding 'praegustata', and it might represent the original version of the chapter heading – possibly restored in the witnesses that contain it in order to resolve a lacunose form that appears to prohibit the eating of meat altogether. It cannot be ruled out, however, that the restoration occurred conjecturally, independently of an Old Latin text, perhaps inspired by passages such as Ezekiel 4:14 (et morticinum et laceratum a bestiis non comedi).
Non credendum facile quod quo audieris

Non consentiendum fieri testis testes
iniquis iniquus iniqui The reading iniquis found in the Turonian Bibles (emended independently by P2) and the Tours Ochtateuch is certainly less appropriate than iniquus; however, the ablative might correspond to the Greek μετὰ τοῦ ἀδίκου in Ex 23:1. It cannot be ruled out that the repetition present in the latter (μετὰ τοῦ ἀδίκου γενέσθαι μάρτυς ἄδικος) was reflected in an Old Latin version (see Lucif. Calar. de Athanas. 1, 3: non consenties cum iniquo fieri testis iniustus) and in the corresponding chapter heading, which perhaps may originally have had a form such as testis iniquus [cum?] iniquis.
Φ Tu (error) vs Am1

Non esse cum multis multos multis
ad in ad See the following note. Φ Tu (error) vs Am1
malam malum malam malum malum malam The feminine malam, although incongruous in terms of meaning, may have been influenced by the expression ἐπὶ κακίᾳ in the Septuagint, but remains unintelligible without a preposition. Φ Tu (error) vs Am1
Non declinare cum multis iudicium
Non spernendum pauperem in iudicio nec pro illo faciendum
Errantem errandum inimici iumentum reuocandum
Inimici iumentum pressum praessum presum pondere erigendum
Iudicium pauperis pauperes pauperis non subuertendum sub//// subuertendum

Ab omni uerbo iniquo in quo uerbo iniquo uerbo
recedendum recendum recedendum -cedendum ex corr. K
Innocentem et iustum non occidendum
Impium non iustificandum et munera non accipienda

De anno annos anno
remissionis remissiones remissionis
septimo VIImus
Sabbato sabbatum non operandum
Omnia quae praecepit deus obseruanda obseruandum observanda ex corr.

Tribus temporibus anni diem sollemnem solemnem solemnem sollemnem
agendam agendum
Diem festum azymorum azimorum obseruandum
Vacuum in conspectu domini meo dei minime apparendum

Tribus temporibus anni omnem omne Omnem vestrum masculinum in the Codex Lugdunensis for Gn 17:10 (the manuscript is lacunose for Ex 23:17); omne masculinum in the Vulgate. See ThLL 8, p. 425, l. 69–73. masculinum parere parare parcere parere domino
Primitias terrae terrȩ offerendas

Non coquendum agnum
agnum in lacte lactae lacte matris suae

Angelum promittit dominus
dominus qui praecedat

Qui deum quidem quod si audierit
liberari libera liberari ab inimicis ab inimicis liberari liberari ab inimicis
possit
Deos alienigenarum alienarum non adorandos adorandas adorandos

Timorem et uespas uespes uespas missurum domini domini dominum dominum Φ (error) vs Am1 Tu qui eiceret eieceret eiceret eieceret eicerit eicerat eierit eiciat
alienigenas alienigines alieniginas alienas
Dixit dominus moysi moysen ascendere ad se in montem cum maioribus natu

Aedificauit Edificauit
Moyses mosises altare ex ex et
duodecim duodecem
lapidibus lapidibus titulos lapidimus
Ascendit Moyses cum maioribus natu
Omnes electi uenerunt in loco loco locum dei

Dicit dominus
dominus Moysi ut ascendat in montem
accipere accire accipere tabulas testamenti
Ascendit ascendat ascendit moyses et descendit discendit ad eum maiestas domini

Dicit dominus offerri initia offerrenitia offerre primitia offerre initia offerri initia
ad
tabernaculum faciendum

app.lem:
De fabrica arcae archae arcȩ et mensae mense
et et
tabernaculi
app.rdg: De fabrica arcae et mensae et tabernaculi

item.title: De fabrica altaris altaris arce et altaris The substitute reading of Sg1 perhaps echoes Ex 38:21 (haec sunt instrumenta tabernaculi testimonii)
De oleo lucernae lucernȩ
De aaron et filiis filios eius ut sacerdotio fungantur fugantur
De e stola sancta
De ueste sacerdotali

De ordinandis ordinantis ordinantis ordinantis sacerdotibus et de
hostia ostia immolanda

app.note: The original chapter heading refers to Ex 30:1–6; the substitute one in Sg1 likely to Ex 30:19 (lavabunt in eo Aaron et filii eius manus suas ac pedes), possibly because the offering of incense is also mentioned in the following chapter heading. However, the ablution of hands and feet by the priests is mentioned in chapter 113.

item.title:
De oblatione odorationis orationis suauissimi odoris For the variant reading in Sg1 see Lv 1:17; 3:5; Nm 15, 10; 28:8 adorationis exortationis exorationis The use of the noun 'odoratio' to refer to incense as in the Turonian Bibles does not appear to be otherwise attested. It cannot be entirely ruled out that the original reading was the one witnessed by the Tours Octateuch, in reference to Ex 30:9–10 in the Codex Lugdunensis: Et non offeretis in eo incensum aliud [...] et exorabit [Vulg.: deprecabitur] Aaron...
Φ (adiaph.?) vs Am1 vs Tu

Sacerdotes lauare manus et pedes cum accedunt accendunt
sacrificare sr̄ sanctuarium
de oleo linitionis linicionis sancto
De unguento ungento unguento ungento unguenti incensi

De beseleel beselehel quem repleuit dominus spiritu spiritum
sapientiae sapientiȩ omnis sapientiae Φ Am1 (adiaph.) vs Tu
ad omne opus adonem ad dei opus ad omnem opus
De sabbato obseruando
Vbi Ibi Vbi dat dominus Moysi tabulas testamenti
Fecit aaron uitulum conflatile conflatilem
Dicit dominus Moysi descende si descende descende uelociter
Orauit moyses pro populo pupulo populo
Vidit moyses uitulum et confregit tabulas
Dicit Dixit Φ Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 moyses ad aaron: Quare hoc fecisti

peccatum eorum peccata eorum

ascendite ascende ascende ascendite in terram quam iuraui iurau iuraui patribus uestris

in Sg1 numbers are prepared before and when reaching the end of the line the rubrics are interrupted

Locutus Loquutus est dominus ad moysen quomodo loquitur quis quas
ad amicum suum
ad amicum suum

Petit fecit moyses uidere dominum et ait illi dominus: Non et ait illi dominus non
potes potest potes potest
uidere faciem meam

uelabat uelabit
faciem suam

die
sabbatorum

et
altaris et
stolam rerum stolam rem stolarem solam rem stolam rerum stolarem stolam stolam
sacerdotalium

Fecerunt beseleel beseel belesehel beselehel
et eliab ooliab eliab ooliab eleeb eleab
et
omnis sapiens omnes sapientes 'omnis sapiens' in the Vulgate and in the Codex Lugdunensis (according to πᾶς σοφὸς in the Septuagint) stolas sanctas

Fecit beseleel beseel arcam testimonii et mensam
et candelabrum et septem VII
lucernas et altare aereum et oleum liniamenti lini/// timiama
feram comestam non manducandam fera comestam non manducandum commixtum non manducandum feram co//mestam non manducandum feram comestam non manducandam feram comestam non manducandum feram comesta non manducandam
I have not been able to find an explanation for the incongruous addition 'feram comestam non manducandam' (recte: -andum), which—albeit with some variants—is shared by both the Maurdramnus Bible and the Tours Octateuch. Its content recalls the problematic chapter 72. According to Henri Quentin (Mémoire sur l’établissement du texte de la Vulgate, Rome–Paris 1922, p. 347), the explanation may lie in omissions and alterations in the order of topics introduced by the Septuagint. Φ vs Am1 vs Tu – all in error? (see critical note)

In die primo mensis mens// mensis mensi mensis primi praecepit praecipit praecipit praecepit praecepit praecipit dominus moysi sanctificare omnia et induere inducere aaron stola stolam
sancta sanctam eius

In primo mense secundo secundo secundi
anno annos anno anno anni profectionis de
ex
aegypto ȩgypto
statuit stauit statuit moyses tabernaculum testimonii

Consummato nonsummato
tabernaculo texit nubs nubes nubis tabernaculum et maiestas domini dei impleuit illud
